title: 🇨🇦 Canada FIRE Calculator
tagline: Retire early, the Canadian way - TFSA, RRSP, CPP/QPP and OAS, modelled properly.
retirementSpending: Desired after-tax annual spending in retirement
estCppTitle: Not sure? Estimate from work history
estOasTitle: Not sure? Estimate from residence years
estRatio: Average earnings while working, as % of the YMPE cap (2026: ~$74,600)
cppEstUsesFireAge: Uses the FIRE age you entered above ({{age}}) — contributions stop that year.
estResidence: Years lived in Canada by 65 (from age 18)
cppEstNote: Approximates the CPP rule: your best 39 years between 18 and 65 count (the dropout removes 17% of the contributory period). Stopping contributions at FIRE dilutes the average - why early retirees rarely get the maximum. After applying, the calculator also remembers your work history: claiming early (say at 60) shortens the divisor to ~35 years, so zero-income years dilute less - handled automatically. The CPP enhancement (post-2019 contributions) is not modelled, so the estimate leans conservative. Check My Service Canada Account for exact figures.
oasEstNote: OAS is residence-based, not work-based: 40 years in Canada after age 18 gives the full amount, fewer years are prorated (common for immigrants).
coupleNote: Accounts and spending are household totals; tax assumes ideal income splitting between spouses.
accountsHint: Enter today's balances, not the balances you expect at FIRE — the projection grows them from your current age.
pensionAnnual: Annual pension at start age ($/yr, today's dollars, before tax)
pensionNote: Enter the lifetime annual amount from your pension statement (already reduced for an early start, if that applies). Have a defined-contribution (DC) plan or a LIRA instead? Add its balance to the RRSP/RRIF field — it projects the same way here. Payments are taxed as eligible pension income: splittable with a spouse and earning the pension income credit at any age. Not modelled: survivor percentages and Quebec's 65+ provincial splitting restriction.
valPensionWindow: Pension start age should be between {{min}} and {{max}}
valBridgeAfter65: The bridge benefit ends at 65 — starting at 65 or later means it never pays out
nonRegYieldHint: Interest, dividends and ETF distributions in a non-registered account are taxed the year they are paid even when auto-reinvested - that is tax drag. In retirement it joins your taxable income; during working years it is taxed at the marginal rate below. Broad equity ETFs distribute ~2%; bonds/GICs more. Set 0 to disable.
mixNote: Presets set a real return and volatility from FP Canada-style assumptions.
feesHint: Every account's return is reduced by this rate. FP Canada assumptions are gross of fees - net returns require subtracting them: broad index ETFs run ~0.2%, bank mutual funds often 2%+, and a 2% MER can turn a 4% withdrawal rate into a 2% one.
cppStartAge: CPP/QPP start age (60–70; QPP up to 72)
prNote: Sale is tax-free (principal residence exemption); proceeds become investable.
ipNote: On sale, half the gain above the ACB is taxed; sales never happen before FIRE. Net rent is taxed as ordinary income and stops the year the property sells; don’t double-count rent inside "annual savings".
success: Your plan works - money lasts to age {{age}}.
whenNever: No FIRE age up to 75 works with the current inputs.
numberAnswer: Your FIRE number: about {{amount}} in investable assets at the moment you retire at {{age}}.
incomeChartNote: Stacked areas are gross cash sources; the dashed line is tax paid. A GIS layer (tax-free supplement) appears from 65 when taxable income is low enough.
taxChartNote: Stacked areas split this year's tax proportionally across taxable components — each slice is that source's share of total taxable income, scaled against the year's actual tax. Not a statutory split (all income is taxed together on one bracket ladder), but the slices always sum to the tax line in the chart above. Hover a point to see total taxable income, the average rate and the marginal bracket (computed per person, since Canada taxes individuals).
strategyNote: Ranked by after-tax estate value: at death the remaining RRSP/RRIF is fully income in the final year (when no spousal rollover is left) and half of unrealized non-registered gains is taxed - strategies that park a large RRSP to the end get taxed heavily here. The RRSP→RRIF conversion itself is not a taxable event.
timingAlready: Your current start ages are already optimal for this plan.
timingNote: Deterministic scan of start ages for the primary person; longevity risk not included.
mcNote: How to read it: the dark line is the median of 1,000 runs; the shaded band spans the 10th–90th percentile - 8 out of 10 runs land inside, and the band's lower edge is close to the "unlucky script". The dashed red line is the actual balance path of the single run that ran out earliest - it drops to zero before the 10th-percentile band does, because at that age fewer than 10% of runs had failed yet; that line is where the "worst run" figure in the text above comes from. Your accounts hold the same market, so every account shares each year's draw (correlation) - splitting money across wrappers does not cancel volatility. Read the success rate as the odds of never needing to adjust: below 100% means some scripts require spending cuts or extra income, not bankruptcy. It is also sensitive to the return assumption - half a percentage point can move it several points, so don't treat single digits as precise. Rules of thumb: ≥85% is solid, 70–85% is tight, <70% suggests lower spending or a later FIRE.
mcIdle: What is Monte Carlo? The charts above assume the same return every year, but real markets have good and bad years. Monte Carlo randomizes each year's return (scaled to your asset-mix volatility) and reruns your entire plan 1,000 times, counting how often the money lasts to life expectancy - that's the success rate. It exposes the big blind spot: a bear market in the first years after FIRE (sequence-of-returns risk) hurts far more than a late one. Click the button to run.
scenarioHint: Save a snapshot, then change inputs (retire later, spend less…) to compare.
disclaimer: Educational estimate only, today's dollars, simplified tax rules - not financial advice.
simplificationsNote: This calculator makes a number of simplifying assumptions — results are an approximation, not a guarantee of reality.
targetLate: Short: about {{amount}} at {{fireAge}} vs target {{target}}; saving on, you reach it at age {{age}}.
targetNever: With current savings and returns, {{target}} is never reached.
targetHint: Includes proceeds from planned property sales (principal residence tax-free, investment-property gain taxed; unsold real estate doesn't count); if short, saving is assumed to continue. Values are end-of-year, matching the chart. Whether the money lasts for life is what the other modes answer.
meltdownNote: The RRSP funds spending first each year, but only as much as spending needs and never beyond the lowest-bracket room left after CPP/OAS (one bracket per spouse) - nothing is withdrawn just to prepay tax. Shortfalls come from non-registered → TFSA; the RRSP remainder rides past 71 and exits via RRIF minimums, staying far below the OAS clawback.
dwzGoalNote: Under Die with Zero, strategies are ranked by sustainable annual spending.
strategyNoteDwz: Ranked by sustainable spending: the highest real annual spending each strategy can sustain to life expectancy, ending near zero - the less tax paid, the more you can spend.
dwzNote: In today's purchasing power - returns are inflation-adjusted, so constant real spending already covers inflation.
dwzRiskWarning: For reference only, not a recommendation to apply directly: this is a zero-margin theoretical ceiling that assumes every year hits the expected return exactly - once Monte Carlo adds real volatility back in, its success rate is often low. Run the Monte Carlo simulation below first to check the real odds before using this number.

rrsp.title: RRSP - Registered Retirement Savings Plan
rrsp.body: Contributions deduct from taxable income now; growth is tax-deferred; withdrawals are fully taxed as income. Best filled at high-income years and drained at low-income years. Must convert to a RRIF by the end of the year you turn 71. For FIRE: the low-income bridge years are the golden window to drain it cheaply (see meltdown).

rrif.title: RRIF - Registered Retirement Income Fund
rrif.body: The RRSP's exit form: the RRSP must convert to a RRIF by the end of age 71 (the conversion itself is tax-free) - but 71 is only the deadline; you can convert earlier to match your retirement timeline, or convert only part of it. From the next year, age-based minimum withdrawals are mandatory (about 5.28% the year you turn 72, rising to 20% at 95): each withdrawal is added to that year's taxable income, and the mandatory minimum is usually exempt from withholding tax - but exempt from withholding isn't the same as tax-free, so this calculator computes the actual tax owed directly regardless of what's withheld. Anything pulled beyond that year's spending is reinvested after tax in a non-registered account. Couples can elect at RRIF setup to use the younger spouse's age for the minimum calculation, deferring tax further (the election is irrevocable) - this calculator applies it automatically, always using the younger spouse's age. An oversized RRSP forces big taxable withdrawals - the core reason to meltdown early.

withholding.title: Withholding tax
withholding.body: A lump-sum RRSP withdrawal has income tax withheld at source in tiers (outside Quebec: 10% up to $5,000, 20% from $5,001-15,000, 30% above $15,000; Quebec adds its own provincial withholding on top of the federal one). This is only a prepayment, not the final bill - at tax time the withdrawal is added to that year's total income and taxed at your marginal rate, with the withheld amount credited against it. A RRIF's mandatory minimum withdrawal is usually exempt from withholding, but it's still fully taxable income. This calculator doesn't model withholding timing at all - it computes the actual tax owed for the year directly, regardless of what was withheld.

tfsa.title: TFSA - Tax-Free Savings Account
tfsa.body: Contributions are after-tax, but growth and withdrawals are completely tax-free, and withdrawn room comes back the next year. The most flexible wrapper in Canada: withdrawals never touch taxable income or trigger OAS clawback. Usually the last account to draw from. In the withdrawal order it always goes last: let the tax-free compounding run longest, keep it as your only zero-tax-consequence cash source late in life, and it passes tax-free at death.

nonreg.title: Non-registered account
nonreg.body: A plain investment account with no tax wrapper: interest is fully taxed, eligible dividends get a credit, and 50% of realized capital gains (sale price minus ACB) is taxable. Where money goes once TFSA and RRSP room is full. In the withdrawal order it is usually spent first (after the RRSP's low-bracket room) - the only wrapper that keeps leaking taxable income, so the longer it sits the more it leaks.

acb.title: ACB - Adjusted Cost Base
acb.body: What you originally paid for your non-registered assets ("book cost" at your broker). Tax applies to 50% of (sale price − ACB). Higher ACB = smaller gain = less tax. Cash and GICs have ACB equal to face value. Entering 0 makes the calculator treat the whole balance as profit and badly overstates tax.

cpp.title: CPP / QPP - Canada / Quebec Pension Plan
cpp.body: The work-based government pension (QPP in Quebec). The amount depends on your best 39 years of contributions between 18 and 65, relative to the YMPE ceiling. Standard start at 65; as early as 60 (−0.6%/month) or as late as 70 (+0.7%/month, +42%). Retiring early dilutes the average - FIRE folks rarely get the maximum.

oas.title: OAS - Old Age Security
oas.body: A residence-based pension, independent of work: 40 years in Canada after age 18 earns the full amount (~$9,024/yr in 2026, +10% automatically at 75), fewer years prorate. Starts at 65, deferrable to 70 (+0.6%/month). High income triggers the clawback.

gic.title: GIC - Guaranteed Investment Certificate
gic.body: A term deposit: locked term, guaranteed rate, no principal risk. Interest is fully taxed annually in a non-registered account, so GICs are more tax-efficient inside a TFSA or RRSP. Not to be confused with GIS (the low-income supplement).

ympe.title: YMPE - Year’s Maximum Pensionable Earnings
ympe.body: The earnings ceiling for CPP/QPP contributions and benefits (~$71,300 in 2025). Earning at or above the YMPE makes that a full-credit year. The CPP estimator’s "% of the cap" refers to this.

meltdown.title: RRSP Meltdown
meltdown.body: Deliberately draining the RRSP during the low-income window after FIRE, keeping each year's taxable income within the lowest bracket (whatever room CPP/OAS leaves), to avoid two future tax events: forced RRIF minimums pushing you into higher brackets, and the remaining balance being taxed at the top rate at death. Note: the RRSP→RRIF conversion itself is tax-free, so there is no need to empty it by 71 - the remainder simply dribbles out via RRIF minimums; over-withdrawing just pays extra tax. The "meltdown ceiling" offers three settings: the lowest bracket (default, most conservative), the second bracket, or the OAS clawback threshold - raising it helps large RRSPs empty faster in the early low-income years, reducing future RRIF-forced withdrawals and the fully-taxable balance at death, at the cost of somewhat more tax paid along the way.

clawback.title: OAS Clawback (recovery tax)
clawback.body: Net income above a threshold ($95,323 in 2026) claws back OAS at 15% of the excess; around $155,000 it reaches zero. Large RRIF withdrawals are a common trigger - avoiding it is one goal of withdrawal-order planning.

fire.title: FIRE - Financial Independence, Retire Early
fire.body: Accumulating enough invested assets that passive income covers living costs, allowing exit from mandatory work before traditional retirement age. The key levers: savings rate, retirement spending, and withdrawal strategy.

dwz.title: Die with Zero
dwz.body: A life choice: rather than maximizing an estate, spend the money while alive - target the highest stable spending that lasts exactly to life expectancy. Under this goal the calculator ranks strategies by sustainable annual spending; less tax paid means more to spend. Note that this "max sustainable spending" is a theoretical ceiling computed assuming every year hits the expected return exactly - it has zero safety margin by construction. Real markets have good years and bad years, and Monte Carlo's success rate for this exact number is often noticeably low - always run Monte Carlo before applying it.

montecarlo.title: Monte Carlo simulation
montecarlo.body: Instead of one fixed return, market returns are randomized each year and the plan is simulated thousands of times, yielding a success rate. It exposes sequence-of-returns risk: a bear market in the first years after FIRE hurts far more than one later.

estate.title: Estate taxation (deemed disposition)
estate.body: Canada has no formal estate tax, but at death assets are deemed sold: the remaining RRSP/RRIF is fully income in the final year (unless rolled to a surviving spouse) and 50% of unrealized non-registered gains is taxed. TFSA and the principal residence pass tax-free. Non-registered holdings and unsold real estate also owe **probate / estate administration fees** (province-dependent — roughly 1.5% in Ontario, 1.4% in BC, up to 1.7% in Nova Scotia (the highest), a few hundred dollars flat in Alberta and Quebec, nothing in Manitoba); RRSP/RRIF/TFSA with a named beneficiary bypass it entirely. That is why after-tax estate value — net of both the death-year tax and probate — is the honest comparison metric.

principalresidence.title: Principal Residence Exemption
principalresidence.body: Capital gains on your primary home are entirely tax-free - one of the largest tax breaks in Canada. In a FIRE plan, downsizing releases a large tax-free lump sum that becomes investable. Investment properties do not qualify.

withdrawalorder.title: Withdrawal order (decumulation strategy)
withdrawalorder.body: Which account you tap first in retirement can swing the after-tax outcome by hundreds of thousands. Rule of thumb, three layers: 1) RRSP first in low-income years, capped within the lowest bracket (see meltdown) - it's tax-deferred, the bill comes eventually, so pay it in your cheapest years; 2) non-registered next - the only wrapper that keeps leaking taxable income, so the longer it sits the more it leaks; 3) TFSA always last: growth is permanently tax-free (highest-quality compounding - let it run longest), withdrawals never push up your bracket or trigger the clawback (your only zero-tax-consequence source of cash late in life, after RRIF minimums fill your brackets), and it passes tax-free at death. The comparison card below quantifies each order with your own numbers.

bridge.title: Bridge period (FIRE → pensions)
bridge.body: The years between FIRE and the start of CPP/OAS: no salary, no benefits yet - the lowest-taxable-income window of your life, and the prime hunting ground for the RRSP meltdown. A longer bridge needs more self-funded cash but gives more room to drain the RRSP at low rates; the 'defer CPP/OAS' advice only works if the bridge is funded.

sequencerisk.title: Sequence-of-returns risk
sequencerisk.body: The same average return with bad years in a different order gives wildly different endings: a bear market in the first years after FIRE forces you to sell assets at the bottom to live, and the damaged principal never recovers; the same bear at 80 is nearly harmless. It is early retirement's biggest hidden risk - invisible in fixed-return charts, and the main thing the Monte Carlo success rate measures.

realdollars.title: Today's purchasing power (real vs nominal)
realdollars.body: This calculator runs entirely in real dollars: every amount is stated in today's purchasing power and returns are net of inflation. The benefit: a number in any year compares directly to your life today - "spending 100k a year" still buys today's 100k worth at 80. Switch to Nominal display to see future face values (rising each year); purchasing power is identical.

splitting.title: Income splitting (couples)
splitting.body: Canada taxes individuals, so under progressive rates the same household income split across two returns pays far less than piled on one (two personal amounts, two runs up the low brackets). Couple mode assumes an ideal 50/50 split. Reality check: before 65, RRSP withdrawals are taxed to the account owner only and cannot be split - an even split during the bridge requires both spouses to hold comparable RRSP balances, which unbalanced couples should fix in advance with spousal RRSP contributions. After 65, the real pension income splitting rule only covers RRIF-type eligible pension income and caps at 50% to a spouse - this calculator treats all taxable income (CPP, rent, non-registered gains, etc.) as ideally splittable, which is more optimistic than the real rules.

marginalrate.title: Marginal tax rate
marginalrate.body: The rate on your next dollar of income - not the average on all of it. Canada's brackets are progressive: only the slice above each threshold pays that bracket's rate. Planning is largely about controlling the marginal rate - the meltdown keeps RRSP draws in the lowest bracket, while the clawback acts as a hidden extra 15% marginal rate at higher incomes. Ontario residents also face the surtax and health premium on top (see the entry); Quebec residents face the RAMQ premium and FSS contribution (see the entry).

correlation.title: Return correlation
correlation.body: Whether two investments move together. Your TFSA, RRSP and non-registered accounts usually hold the same markets - in a bear market they fall together, so spreading money across account wrappers diversifies nothing. The Monte Carlo here applies the same yearly market draw to every account precisely so wrappers aren't mistaken for diversification (that mistake would inflate the success rate). What actually lowers volatility is the asset mix (stocks vs bonds), not the account shell.

mer.title: MER - management expense ratio
mer.body: The share of assets a fund silently deducts every year (you never see a bill, but it is real). Broad index ETFs charge ~0.05-0.25%; actively managed bank mutual funds commonly 1.5-2.5%. Over 30 years a 2% fee consumes roughly forty percent of the final portfolio. This calculator treats return assumptions as gross of fees and subtracts the rate you enter separately.

taxdrag.title: Tax drag
taxdrag.body: The non-registered account is the only wrapper that leaks tax: interest, dividends and yearly ETF distributions are taxed even if you withdraw nothing (foreign dividends also lose a 15% withholding tax), steadily eroding compound growth. TFSA/RRSP growth is untouched - which is why withdrawal-order wisdom says not to let non-registered money linger. The calculator adds your distribution yield to each year’s taxable income; reinvested distributions raise the ACB so they are not taxed again at sale.

agecredit.title: Age amount
agecredit.body: A tax credit for those 65+: the federal amount is up to $9,208 in 2026 (reduced at 15% of net income above ~$46,400, gone near $107,800), with provincial amounts on top (ON $6,342, BC $5,927, AB $6,345; Quebec folds it into a combined family-tested retirement credit). It matters for meltdown planning: keeping taxable income under the phase-out start saves each person well over a thousand dollars a year. The calculator applies it automatically from 65.

pensioncredit.title: Pension income credit
pensioncredit.body: The first $2,000 of eligible pension income each year earns a federal credit (provinces add their own amounts). Employer pension annuities qualify at any age; RRIF withdrawals only from 65 — which is why planners suggest drawing at least $2,000 a year from a RRIF after 65: that slice is nearly tax-free. RRSP withdrawals never qualify at any age. The calculator applies the credit automatically: employer pensions at any age, RRSP/RRIF draws from 65.

gis.title: GIS - Guaranteed Income Supplement
gis.body: A tax-free top-up for low-income seniors (up to ~$13,500/yr for a single in 2026), paid only while receiving OAS and income-tested: roughly 50-60 cents lost per dollar of taxable income, reaching zero near $22,800 (single). A hidden benefit for FIRE folks: the income test excludes OAS itself, and TFSA withdrawals are completely invisible to it - an early retiree living on TFSA and already-taxed capital after 65 can report near-zero income and collect the full amount. Capturing it requires the RRSP to be essentially empty before 65 (RRIF minimums would eat the GIS) and usually pairs with deferring CPP to 70. Not to be confused with a GIC. Couples with an age gap get an often-missed extra: if your spouse already receives GIS, the 60-64-year-old partner not yet OAS-eligible can claim the Allowance - also tax-free, income-tested on combined household income, worth up to about $1,428/month in 2026 and phasing to zero near $41,600 combined income; the calculator applies it automatically.

surtax.title: Ontario surtax & health premium
surtax.body: Ontario's two stealth add-ons: (1) the surtax - once provincial tax itself passes ~$5,800 it is increased by 20%, and by a further 36% past ~$7,400, effectively multiplying high-income provincial marginal rates by 1.2-1.56x; (2) the Ontario Health Premium - up to $900 per person per year, tiered on taxable income. Both settle on the tax return; they are why Ontario feels more expensive than its bracket table suggests. The calculator applies both automatically for ON residents.

rent.title: Rental income (taxation)
rent.body: When an investment property is kept for its cash flow instead of sold, the net rent (rent minus property tax, insurance, maintenance, management and other operating costs) is 100% taxable as ordinary income — no 50% capital-gains discount, no dividend credit. Enter the net figure here: during accumulation it is taxed at your working marginal rate and saved into the non-registered account; in retirement it joins taxable income and counts against tax brackets, the OAS clawback and the GIS income test. The sell-vs-hold trade-off: holding keeps the appreciation but the income is heavily taxed and the accrued gain is still taxed at death; selling crystallizes the gain once (half taxable) in exchange for rebalanceable financial assets.

baristafire.title: Barista FIRE — semi-retirement
baristafire.body: FIRE without fully stopping: quit the main career and cover part of your spending with part-time work, a small business or odd projects, so the portfolio only funds the gap. The leverage is dramatic — $20k/yr less drawn from the portfolio is, by the 4% rule, roughly $500k less capital you need to save, often years of retirement gained. The income is taxed as ordinary income, but at retirement-level income the marginal rate is low; after 65 it also enjoys the GIS work exemption (the first $5,000 doesn’t count against the income test, and only half of the next $10,000 does). A sibling concept, Coast FIRE: once compounding alone will reach your number, stop saving and earn just enough to cover current expenses. This calculator models it as a fixed pre-tax annual amount over an age range starting no earlier than FIRE. In couple mode it's taxed entirely on you, not split with your spouse the way an RRSP withdrawal or rent can be — employment-type income can't legally be shared — and only your own OAS is subject to its clawback.

debt.title: Debt amortization and inflation
debt.body: Mortgage and loan payments are contractually fixed in nominal dollars: the same paper amount every year. This calculator works in today’s purchasing power throughout, so the real burden of that same payment shrinks year after year — at 2% inflation, the final payment of a 25-year mortgage weighs only ~61% of today’s. That is the honest mechanism behind "inflation pays your mortgage"; ignoring it overstates debt pressure and pushes your FIRE age out. The engine back-solves the implied interest rate from (balance, annual payment, years remaining) and amortizes year by year; expressed in real dollars, retirement-phase payments add to the spending target and the outstanding balance is subtracted from net worth. Note the semantics of "annual savings": it is what you save after debt payments, so a loan paid off before FIRE affects the balance sheet, not retirement cash flow. A mortgage can also be attached directly to a specific property instead of this general list — the sale then automatically pays it off from the proceeds first, and while held, its interest (not principal) is deductible against that property's rental income, matching how leveraged rentals actually work. Enter each mortgage in one place only, not both.

qclevies.title: RAMQ premium + FSS contribution (Quebec retirees)
qclevies.body: Two easily-missed costs for Quebec retirees, settled on the tax return: (1) the RAMQ prescription-drug-insurance premium — anyone without private drug coverage (the common case once you leave an employer, which is most FIRE'd retirees) defaults onto the public plan and pays for it based on reported income, up to about $770/person/year in 2026; (2) the FSS (Fonds des services de santé) contribution — levied on retirement, investment and capital-gains income (OAS excluded), 1% of the amount over roughly $18,500 up to a $150 cap, then another 1% past roughly $64,355 up to a $1,000 cap. Both are per person and settle in one lump sum at tax time; the calculator applies them automatically for QC residents. Assumption: no private drug plan after retirement (true for most FIRE'd retirees who lose employer benefits) — if you keep private coverage, your actual RAMQ premium is zero.

simplifications.title: What this calculator simplifies
simplifications.body: To keep the math tractable and auditable, this calculator makes a number of simplifications; the results are a directionally honest approximation, not a guarantee of your actual future tax outcome. Couple mode assumes CPP, RRSP withdrawals, rental income and investment income can all be split 50/50 — in reality, RRSP withdrawals before 65 are taxed only to the account owner (unless you've set up a spousal RRSP ahead of time), Barista FIRE side income can't legally be split with a spouse (the calculator already handles this case separately), and the estate tax bill is estimated as an even split between spouses rather than the survivor's own single-filer bracket (usually heavier). Non-registered distributions and net rent are taxed as ordinary income every year (no dividend gross-up/credit, no rental CCA depreciation modelled); GIS and the 60-64 spousal Allowance use a linear approximation of the official tables; TFSA/RRSP contribution room isn't checked and RRSP refunds aren't automatically reinvested; CPP enhancement (post-2019 contributions) isn't modelled, understating younger users' benefits (a conservative direction); debts and mortgages are treated as fixed nominal payments with no refinancing or variable rates; Monte Carlo draws one shared market outcome per year across all accounts (full correlation), when real-world correlation is somewhat lower (also conservative); large one-off costs like long-term care aren't modelled; and some Quebec RAMQ/FSS figures and provincial probate-fee rates are approximated from secondary sources rather than official year-by-year data, since the official sites block automated fetching. Employer pensions add their own simplifications: survivor percentages aren't modelled (both spouses are assumed to reach the shared life expectancy), Quebec's provincial rule that pension splitting requires age 65+ is ignored (splitting stays idealized at any age), a partially-indexed pension only erodes from its start age (deferral-period erosion isn't modelled), and DC/LIRA balances entered in the RRSP field ignore LIF withdrawal caps.

dbpension.title: Employer pension (DB/DC)
dbpension.body: Workplace registered pension plans come in two kinds. Defined benefit (DB): a formula — often 2% × years of service × best-5-year average salary — pays a lifetime annuity, sometimes indexed to CPI, sometimes with a bridge benefit to 65. Defined contribution (DC): an investment account; enter its balance in the RRSP/RRIF field instead of here. Leaving long before retirement (the FIRE case) usually offers a choice between a deferred pension and transferring the commuted value into a locked-in account (LIRA) — tax law caps the sheltered transfer, and the excess is paid out as taxable cash. Pension payments are fully taxable, count toward the OAS clawback and the GIS income test, but are 'eligible pension income' at any age: splittable 50/50 with a spouse and earning the pension income credit.

bridgebenefit.title: Bridge benefit
bridgebenefit.body: A temporary top-up many DB pensions pay from the pension start age until 65, roughly standing in for the CPP/OAS you can't collect yet. It stops at 65 regardless of when your CPP actually starts — plan for the income step-down. Not to be confused with the bridge period (the FIRE-to-government-benefits years).

targetReachedEarly: On track: the {{target}} target is first reached at age {{age}}; by {{fireAge}} (FIRE) you hold about {{amount}}.
targetReachedAtFire: On track: the {{target}} target is reached right at {{fireAge}} (the FIRE year), with about {{amount}}.
whenAssets: Simulating a FIRE at {{age}}, you'd hold about {{amount}} in investments at that year's end.
whenExplain: Method: for each age starting from today, the plan is fully simulated as if you FIREd that year - with your withdrawal strategy, property-sale plan and CPP/OAS start ages - through age {{life}}. An age counts as feasible only if the after-tax spending of {{spending}} is covered every single year without the money running out; shown is the first age that passes.
timingWhy: Why the start age matters: taking CPP early discounts it (starting at 60 pays only 64% of the age-65 amount) and deferring boosts it (142% at 70); OAS starts no earlier than 65 and grows to 136% at 70. But waiting means a longer bridge funded from your own assets. Which wins is not obvious - the tables below fully simulate each choice through age {{life}}.
timingCppTitle: CPP/QPP: impact of each start age (everything else unchanged)
timingOasTitle: OAS: impact of each start age (everything else unchanged)
timingBestCombo: Best combination from the joint scan: CPP at {{cpp}} + OAS at {{oas}}.
displayModeNote: All amounts are today's purchasing power - the engine uses inflation-adjusted real returns, so constant real spending already keeps pace with inflation; this toggle only changes how numbers are displayed, not the calculation itself.
nominalNote: Nominal mode: every amount is converted to future face value at {{pct}}%/yr inflation - withdrawals and benefits rise each year, but purchasing power is identical to Real mode; the math itself always runs in real dollars.
numberHave: With current savings and your property plan, you are projected to hold about {{amount}} at {{age}} -
numberExplain: Method: a binary search for the minimum investable assets at age {{age}} that lets the plan - with your withdrawal strategy, property-sale plan and CPP/OAS settings - spend through age {{life}} without running out. Assets are assumed split like your current TFSA/RRSP/non-registered mix (account tax treatment matters). Because government benefits and the home sale are counted, this is usually far below the crude "25× spending" rule.
yearTableNote: Where each year's money comes from, how much tax, at which bracket - audit-grade detail. Amounts are always today's purchasing power (brackets are defined in real terms); taxable income is per person after income splitting; marginal rate = federal + provincial statutory bracket (QC abatement applied). Rows marked ! have a spending shortfall. From 65 the tax math automatically includes the age amount and pension income credit (the age amount shrinks as income rises).
mcFailStats: Anatomy of the unlucky scripts: {{count}} runs failed ({{pct}}%) - the worst went broke at {{earliest}}, the typical failed run at {{median}}.
mcFailWhy: Failed runs averaged {{failed}}%/yr over the first 5 post-FIRE years, versus {{succeeded}}% for successful runs - the unlucky script is almost always the same one: a bear market right after retiring forces selling at the bottom to fund spending, and the damaged principal never recovers (sequence-of-returns risk). Late bear markets barely hurt.
mcNoFail: All {{n}} runs lasted to life expectancy - no failing scripts.
privacyNote: All financial inputs stay in your browser and are never uploaded. Anonymous usage statistics (page views, feature clicks) are collected via Google Analytics.
valBanner: {{count}} input error(s) — results below can’t be trusted until fixed
valFireBeforeCurrent: FIRE age can’t be below your current age
valLifeBeforeFire: Life expectancy can’t be below the FIRE age
valCppWindow: Claim age must be between {{min}} and {{max}}
valOasWindow: Claim age must be between {{min}} and {{max}}
valOasMax: Above the official full amount at 65 (${{max}})
valSplitSum: Split adds up to {{sum}}%, should be 100%
valBookExceeds: Book value exceeds the non-registered balance (engine caps it)
valAcbAboveValue: ACB above market value — a negative gain is taxed as zero
valSellBeforeFire: A sale before the FIRE age is deferred to the FIRE year
valSellInPast: Sale age is in the past; treated as an immediate sale
extraIncomeNote: Part-time work, a small business, selling things — taxed as ordinary income; the GIS income test exempts the first $5,000 of work income plus half of the next $10,000. Don’t subtract it from "retirement spending" yourself.
valExtraToBeforeFrom: End age can’t be before the start age
valExtraBeforeFire: Years before FIRE are ignored (side income starts at FIRE)
debtNote: Payments are fixed in nominal dollars — inflation lightens their real weight every year; the engine derives the implied interest rate from (balance, payment, years) and amortizes accordingly. In retirement, payments add to the spending target until paid off; "annual savings" is assumed to already be net of debt payments.
valDebtUnpayable: Payment × years is less than the balance — this loan never amortizes; check the three numbers
mortgageInterestNote: This mortgage is discharged straight from the sale proceeds when the property sells; while held, its interest portion (not principal) is deductible against the rental income. The payment still counts toward the spending target — don't also enter this loan under "Debts" above.
